section: Chapter 1: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years

questions.question: Who was Al-Idrisi and what was unique about his map?
questions.correct_answer: An Arab geographer; his 1154 CE map showed South India at the top and Sri Lanka as an island above it
questions.explanation: This differs from modern maps where North is at the top, showing how geographical perspectives changed over time.

questions.question: What is 'Cartography'?
questions.correct_answer: The science or art of map-making
questions.explanation: Cartography changed significantly between the 12th and 18th centuries as travelers and traders provided more information.

questions.question: How did the meaning of 'Hindustan' differ between the 13th century and today?
questions.correct_answer: In the 13th century, it meant lands under the Delhi Sultan; today it means the modern nation-state of India
questions.explanation: Minhaj-i-Siraj used it to describe Punjab and Haryana, whereas today it encompasses the entire country.

questions.question: What is an 'Archive'?
questions.correct_answer: A place where manuscripts and historical documents are stored
questions.explanation: Archives are used by governments and institutions to preserve old official records and transactions.

questions.question: Why did 'Scribes' introduce changes in manuscripts?
questions.correct_answer: Because they copied manuscripts by hand and often made small errors or intentional adjustments
questions.explanation: Over centuries, these small changes grew so much that different versions of the same text became substantially different.

questions.question: Name three new technologies that appeared in the subcontinent between 700 and 1750.
questions.correct_answer: The Persian wheel, the spinning wheel, and firearms
questions.explanation: The Persian wheel was used for irrigation, the spinning wheel for weaving, and firearms in combat.

questions.question: Who were the 'Rajputs' and what qualities were attributed to them?
questions.correct_answer: A group claiming Kshatriya status; known for their extreme valor and sense of loyalty
questions.explanation: The term is derived from 'Rajaputra' (son of a ruler) and applied to warriors and rulers.

questions.question: What are 'Jatis' and how were they regulated?
questions.correct_answer: Sub-castes grouped by background and occupation; regulated by 'Jati Panchayats'
questions.explanation: Jatis framed their own rules to manage the conduct of their members in addition to following village customs.

questions.question: What is a 'Pan-regional' empire?
questions.correct_answer: An empire that spans across diverse and many regions
questions.explanation: Examples include the empires of the Cholas, Khaljis, Tughluqs, and Mughals.

questions.question: How did British historians divide Indian history in the mid-19th century?
questions.correct_answer: Into three periods: Hindu, Muslim, and British
questions.explanation: This division assumed the religion of the rulers was the only significant historical change.
questions.source_section: Thinking about Time and Historical Periods

notes.title: Periodization
notes.content: Modern historians usually look at economic and social factors to characterize different moments of the past rather than just the religion of rulers.
notes.source_section: Thinking about Time and Historical Periods

notes.title: New Foods
notes.content: Potatoes, corn, chillies, tea, and coffee were new crops that arrived in the subcontinent during this millennium.

notes.title: Foreigner (Medieval context)
notes.content: In the medieval period, a 'foreigner' was any stranger who was not part of a particular society or culture, unlike today where it means a non-Indian.

facts.fact: Ziyauddin Barani was a 14th-century chronicler who wrote his first chronicle in 1356.
Paper became cheaper and more widely available in the 14th century, leading to an increase in written records.
The thousand years between 700 and 1750 were a time of great mobility, where groups of people traveled long distances in search of fortune.
